metadata.title:
MEDALS INSTEAD


0F-CAsH PRIZES

DRY FARMING CONGRESS DI.
RECTORS DISCUSS CHANGE
IN PLAN OF MAKING


AWARDS.

pages.raw_text:
Gold, silver and bronze medals
and diplomas may take the place OF
small cash prizes on the premium
list OF the International Dry Farm.
ing exposition in connection with
the fifth Dry Farming congress in
Spokane October to 8. Members
or the nittee on premiums an
nounced at a meeting of the Wash.
lngton board of control yesterda'-y
that they are considering the abal
donment of the plan of offering a
large number of cash prizes and rib
bons as first, second and third
premiums in the general classes
and substituting medals and d.
plomas.


For an exposition of the scope
and Importance of the Dry Farming
congress" said J. X. Smith, a men,
Der of the committee in discussing
the question, small cash prizes
have no real significance. A dollar
or two is no inducement for any
farmer to put forth special effort to
prepare and install an exhibit, espe-
cially when he has to bring his ex-
hibit several hundred or even
thousand miles, as will be the case
in many instances at this exposi-
ton. What he comes here for is to
win premium and we do not want
to give the exhibitors the impress
sion that we are running an inter-
national exposition on a one dollar
basis.


"The intrinsic value of the prem
iums is a secondary consideration,
whether they are first in the sweep
stakes classes or third in the most
insignificant lots Of the general
classes. We believe that If we offer
medals and diplomas we will get
better exhibits and more of them.
A medal represents something. No
matter whether it is first, second
or third premium, it is trophy of
at least some degree of victory in
an international competition and
will be taken home by the. winner
and hung among his heirlooms and
priced for the honor it represents"


Several members of the board ex-
pressed themselves in favor of the
medal idea and said it would prob
ably be adopted.
